questions.subject: English
questions.examName: English and Logical Reasoning Prep Guide
questions.q:
Of the given four pairs three pairs express the relationship similar to that expressed in the capitalised pair. Select that pair that is not related in this way.

TEPID: HOT::?
questions.solution:
In the given words, the first one is the milder form of the second one. Tepid means slightly warm and hot means high temperature.
Winter is a cold season and summer is the warmest season.
Hence, this option is the correct answer.
questions.source: English and Logical Reasoning Prep Guide

questions.subject: English
questions.examName: English and Logical Reasoning Prep Guide
questions.q:
In each question below, are given two sentences numbered I and II. In these sentences, two homonyms are given in italics, which may be either misspelled or inappropriate in the context of the sentences. Read both the sentences carefully and decide on their correctness on the basis of the italicised words.

I. I think this electric metre is faulty.

II. I require two meters of cloth for this purpose.
options: Both the sentences I and II are correct
I as well II are incorrect, but both could be made correct by interchanging the italicised words
questions.solution:
Both sentences are incorrect.
In the first sentence, the word 'metre' is incorrect. It should be 'meter' which means equipment used to measure electricity, voltage, etc.
In the second sentence, the word 'meters' is incorrect. It should be 'metre' which is the SI unit of length.
It can be observed that both sentences can be corrected by interchanging the italicised words.
questions.source: English and Logical Reasoning Prep Guide

questions.subject: English
questions.examName: English and Logical Reasoning Prep Guide
questions.q:
In each question below, are given two sentences numbered I and II. In these sentences, two homonyms are given in italics, which may be either misspelled or inappropriate in the context of the sentences. Read both the sentences carefully and decide on their correctness on the basis of the italicised words.

I. This drawer is the depositary of my private letters.

II. Nowadays, it is not wise to be depository of other's valuables.
options: Both the sentences I and II are correct
I as well II are incorrect, but both could be made correct by interchanging the italicised words
questions.solution:
Both sentences are incorrect.
In the first sentence, the word 'depositary' is incorrect. It should be 'depository' which means a particular place where something is deposited.
In the second sentence, the word 'depository' is incorrect. It should be 'depositary' which means a person with whom a thing is deposited.
Hence, this option is the correct answer.
questions.source: English and Logical Reasoning Prep Guide

questions.subject: English
questions.examName: English and Logical Reasoning Prep Guide
questions.q:
In each question below, are given two sentences numbered I and II. In these sentences, two homonyms are given in italics, which may be either misspelt or inappropriate in the context of the sentences. Read both the sentences carefully and decide on their correctness on the basis of the italicised words.

I. A painter is an artiste.
II. Raj Kapoor is a skilled artist.
options: Both the sentences I and II are correct
I as well II are incorrect, but both could be made correct by interchanging the italicised words
questions.solution:
Both the given sentences are incorrect.
The first sentence is 'A painter is an artiste'. The word 'artiste' used is incorrect as artiste means a person who is skilled in theatre, singing, dancing, or acting.
The second sentence is 'Raj Kapoor is a skilled artist'. The word 'artist' used here is incorrect as it means a person skilled in fine arts such as poetry or drawing.
If both the italicised words are interchanged then the sentence will become correct.
questions.source: English and Logical Reasoning Prep Guide

questions.subject: English
questions.examName: English and Logical Reasoning Prep Guide
questions.q:
Choose the option which contains a pair of words related to each other in the same way as the pair given in capital letters.

LOYALTY : TRAITOR ::?
questions.solution:
In the above-given words, the first one is the antonym of the second one. Traitor means someone who is not loyal or betrays someone.
Similarly, a liar is a person who is not truthful in his/her actions. Hence, option A is the correct answer.
questions.source: English and Logical Reasoning Prep Guide

questions.subject: English
questions.examName: English and Logical Reasoning Prep Guide
questions.q:
Choose the option which contains a pair of words related to each other in the same way as the pair given in capital letters.

FRAGILE : CRACK ::?
questions.solution:
The given words are FRAGILE : CRACK. Fragile means something that is prone to break easily. If it is fragile then it will crack. Therefore, the first one is the property while the other one is the effect.
Pliable means something that can bend. Hence, PLIABLE is the property while BEND is the effect.
questions.source: English and Logical Reasoning Prep Guide

questions.subject: English
questions.examName: English and Logical Reasoning Prep Guide
questions.q: Two words have identical sound and spelling but not related in meaning. It is known as:
questions.solution:
In linguistics, homonyms, broadly defined, are words which are homographs or homophones, or both.
Two words have identical sound and spelling but not related in meaning. It is known as homonymy.
questions.source: English and Logical Reasoning Prep Guide

questions.subject: English
questions.examName: English and Logical Reasoning Prep Guide
questions.q:
In the following question, select the related word pair from the given alternatives:

Tuberculosis: Lungs :: Typhoid : ?
questions.solution:
\(\begin{array}{|c|c|}

\hline \text { Disease } & \text { Affected Organs } \\

\hline \text { Tuberculosis, Pneumonia } & \text { Lungs } \\

\hline \text { Hepatitis (Jaundice) } & \text { Liver } \\

\hline \text { Typhoid } & \text { Intestine } \\

\hline \text { Rabies } & \text { Brain } \\

\hline

\end{array}\)

Hence, the correct answer is 'Intestine'.
questions.source: English and Logical Reasoning Prep Guide

questions.subject: English
questions.examName: English and Logical Reasoning Prep Guide
questions.q:
Choose the option which contains a pair of words related to each other in the same way as the pair given in capital letters.

FRAGILE : CRACK ::?
questions.solution:
The given words are FRAGILE : CRACK. Fragile means something that is prone to break easily. If it is fragile then it will crack. Therefore, the first one is the property while the other one is the effect.

Pliable means something that can bend. Hence, PLIABLE is the property while BEND is the effect.
questions.source: English and Logical Reasoning Prep Guide

questions.subject: English
questions.examName: English and Logical Reasoning Prep Guide
questions.q:
Of the given four pairs three pairs express the relationship similar to that expressed in the capitalised pair. Select that pair that is not related in this way.

WHISPER: SPEAK::?
questions.solution:
In the given words, the first one is the milder action of the second one. Whispering means to speak softly or in a low voice.
To 'listen' means to pay attention to what somebody is saying. To 'bear' means to deal with or accept something very unpleasant.
Hence, option A is the correct answer.
questions.source: English and Logical Reasoning Prep Guide

questions.subject: English
questions.examName: English and Logical Reasoning Prep Guide
questions.q:
In each question below, are given two sentences numbered I and II. In these sentences, two homonyms are given in italics, which may be either misspelled or inappropriate in the context of the sentences. Read both the sentences carefully and decide on their correctness on the basis of the italicised words.

I. This is the best stationary shop in our locality.

II. I saw a stationery van there.
options: Both the sentences I and II are correct
I as well II are incorrect, but both could be made correct by interchanging the italicised words
questions.solution:
Both sentences are incorrect.
In the first sentence, the word 'stationary' is incorrect. It should be 'stationery' which means shops that contain writing materials such as paper, pen, envelopes, etc.
In the second sentence, the word 'stationery' is incorrect. It should be 'stationary' which is an adjective used for not moving objects.
It can be observed that both sentences can be corrected by interchanging the italicised words.
questions.source: English and Logical Reasoning Prep Guide

questions.subject: English
questions.examName: English and Logical Reasoning Prep Guide
questions.q:
In each question below, are given two sentences numbered I and II. In these sentences, two homonyms are given in italics, which may be either misspelt or inappropriate in the context of the sentences. Read both the sentences carefully and decide on their correctness on the basis of the italicised words.

I. He practices what he preaches.

II. The practise of closing shops on Sundays is quite common.
options: Both the sentences I and II are correct
Neither I nor II is correct and the sentence could not be made correct by interchanging the italicised words
questions.solution:
Both sentences I and II are incorrect.
Practise is a verb and practice is a noun.
In sentence I, the word practises will be used that is a verb. Here, it means that the person is doing the same things that he tells other people to do.
In sentence II, the word practice will be used that is a noun. Here, closing shops on Sundays is a custom.
Hence, option E is the correct answer.
questions.source: English and Logical Reasoning Prep Guide

questions.subject: English
questions.examName: English and Logical Reasoning Prep Guide
questions.q: A word with the same spelling and same pronunciations as another, but with a different meaning is/an:
questions.solution: In linguistics, homonyms, broadly defined, are words which are homographs or homophones or both.
questions.source: English and Logical Reasoning Prep Guide

questions.subject: English
questions.examName: English and Logical Reasoning Prep Guide
questions.q:
Choose the option which contains a pair of words related to each other in the same way as the pair given in capital letters.

LOYALTY : TRAITOR ::?
questions.solution:
In the above-given words, the first one is the antonym of the second one. Traitor means someone who is not loyal or betrays someone.

Similarly, a liar is a person who is not truthful in his/her actions. Hence, option A is the correct answer.
questions.source: English and Logical Reasoning Prep Guide

questions.subject: English
questions.examName: English and Logical Reasoning Prep Guide
questions.q:
Select the most appropriate homonym to fill in the blank.
Please \(\_\_\_\_\) more sugar to the juice so that it becomes sweeter.
questions.solution:
- "Add" means to join something to something else so as to increase the size, number, or amount, which is what is intended in the sentence.
- Example: Please add more sugar to the recipe to enhance the flavour.
questions.source: English and Logical Reasoning Prep Guide

questions.subject: English
questions.examName: English and Logical Reasoning Prep Guide
questions.q:
In each question below, are given two sentences numbered I and II. In these sentences, two homonyms are given in italics, which may be either misspelled or inappropriate in the context of the sentences. Read both the sentences carefully and decide on their correctness on the basis of the italicised words.

I. You should not interfere in one's personal affairs.

II. The personel of ICS were proud of their positions during the British rule in India.
options: Both the sentences I and II are correct
I as well II are incorrect, but both could be made correct by interchanging the italicised words
questions.solution:
Only the first sentence is correct. The word 'personal' is correct. It means something that belongs to only one person.
In the second sentence, the word 'personel' is incorrect. It should be 'personnel' which is used for the people who work for an organisation or army.
Hence, option A is the correct answer.
questions.source: English and Logical Reasoning Prep Guide

questions.subject: English
questions.examName: English and Logical Reasoning Prep Guide
questions.q: Which of the given analogies is correct?
questions.solution:
The correct analogy is:

Canada: Ottawa.

Explanation:

1. Australia: Vienna Incorrect. The capital of Australia is Canberra, not Vienna (which is the capital of Austria).

2. Canada: Ottawa

Correct. The capital of Canada is Ottawa.

3. Chile: Beijing Incorrect. The capital of Chile is Santiago, not Beijing (which is the capital of China).

4. Croatia: Split

Incorrect. The capital of Croatia is Zagreb, not Split (which is a prominent city in Croatia).

5. Egypt: Giza

Incorrect. The capital of Egypt is Cairo, not Giza (which is famous for the Great Pyramids).

Thus, Canada: Ottawa is the only correct analogy.
questions.source: English and Logical Reasoning Prep Guide

questions.subject: English
questions.examName: English and Logical Reasoning Prep Guide
questions.q:
In the question given below, there are two sentences numbered I and II. In both sentences, a homonym is given in italics, which may be either misspelled or inappropriate in the context of the sentence. Read both the sentences carefully and decide on their correctness on the basis of the italicised words.

I.  An ordnance declaring President's rule in the state has been issued today.

II. The ordinance depot is situated far away from this city.
options: Both the sentences I and II are correct
I as well II are incorrect, but both could be made correct by interchanging the italicised words
questions.solution:
Both sentences are incorrect.
In the first sentence, the word 'ordnance' is used which is incorrect. It should be 'ordinance' which means rules and regulations made by a person in authority or government.
In the second sentence, the word 'ordinance' is used which is incorrect. It should be 'ordnance' which means military supplies that consist of equipment and weapons.
It can be observed that both sentences can be corrected by interchanging the italicised words.
questions.source: English and Logical Reasoning Prep Guide

questions.subject: English
questions.examName: English and Logical Reasoning Prep Guide
questions.q:
In the question below, there are two sentences numbered I and II. In these sentences, two homophones are given in italics, which may be either misspelled or inappropriate in the context of the sentences. Read both the sentences carefully and decide on their correctness on the basis of the italicised words.

I. His manners are urban.

II. People of urbane area are civil in manners.
options: Both the sentences I and II are correct.
I as well II are incorrect, but both could be made correct by interchanging the italicised words.
questions.solution:
Both sentences are incorrect.
In the first sentence, the word 'urban' is incorrect. It should be 'urbane' which means to appear relaxed and confident in social behaviour.
In the second sentence, the word 'urbane' is incorrect. It should be 'urban' which means a person living in a town or city.
It can be observed that both sentences can be corrected by interchanging the italicised words.
questions.source: English and Logical Reasoning Prep Guide

questions.subject: English
questions.examName: English and Logical Reasoning Prep Guide
questions.q:
Choose the option which contains a pair of words related to each other in the same way as the pair given in capital letters.

HANDCUFFS : PRISONER ::?
questions.solution:
In the pair of words given above, the first one is the object used to restrain the second one. Handcuffs are used as a restraint device to secure the hands of the prisoner close to each other.

Similarly, a leash is used on a dog to restrain it from running away. Hence, the correct answer is 'Leash : Dog'.
questions.source: English and Logical Reasoning Prep Guide

questions.subject: English
questions.examName: English and Logical Reasoning Prep Guide
questions.q:
In the question below, there are two sentences numbered I and II. In these sentences, two homophones are given in italics, which may be either misspelt or inappropriate in the context of the sentences. Read both the sentences carefully and decide on their correctness on the basis of the italicised words.

I. I loathe flattery.

II. He was loath to go with Rahul.
options: Both the sentences I and II are correct.
I as well II are incorrect, but both could be made correct by interchanging the italicised words.
questions.solution:
Both the given sentences are correct.
In first sentence, loathe is used which means to hate or dislike someone.
In the second sentence, 'loath' is used, which means someone who is unwilling to do something.
Hence, this option is the correct answer.
questions.source: English and Logical Reasoning Prep Guide

questions.subject: English
questions.examName: English and Logical Reasoning Prep Guide
questions.q:
In each question below, are given two sentences numbered I and II. In these sentences, two homonyms are given in italics, which may be either misspelt or inappropriate in the context of the sentences. Read both the sentences carefully and decide on their correctness on the basis of the italicised words.

I. The tenur of his speech was not clear.

II. His teneur of office is drawing to a close.
options: Both the sentences I and II are correct
Neither I nor II is correct and the sentence could not be made correct by interchanging the italicised words
questions.solution:
In the sentence I, 'tenur' is incorrect. It should be tenor. It means the voice of a man.
In the II sentence, the word 'teneur' is also incorrect. It should be 'tenure' that means a legal time period to work at a place or live.
Hence, this option is the correct answer.
questions.source: English and Logical Reasoning Prep Guide

questions.subject: English
questions.examName: English and Logical Reasoning Prep Guide
questions.q:
In each question below, are given two sentences numbered I and II. In these sentences, two homonyms are given in italics, which may be either misspelled or is inappropriate in the context of the sentences. Read both the sentences carefully and decide on their correctness on the basis of the italicized words.

I. You should talk to your boss in a revrent manner.

II. Our revrend Principal is coming.
options: Both the sentences I and II are correct
Neither I nor II is correct, and the sentences could not be made correct by interchanging the italicised words
questions.solution:
Both sentences are incorrect.
In the first sentence, the word 'revrent' is incorrect. It should be 'reverent' which is an adjective used for a person who shows respect towards someone.
In the second sentence, the word 'revrend' is incorrect. It should be 'reverend' which is also an adjective used for a respectable person.
Hence, this option is the correct answer.
questions.source: English and Logical Reasoning Prep Guide

questions.subject: English
questions.examName: English and Logical Reasoning Prep Guide
questions.q:
Of the given four pairs three pairs express the relationship similar to that expressed in the capitalised pair. Select that pair that is related in this way.

TEPID: HOT::?
questions.solution: A similarity between like features of two things, on which a comparison may be based. Hence, the correct option is (2) which are the analogous choice. When something is slightly warm it is called tepid, and extremely warm is called hot, similarly a gentle touch is called a pat while a strong violent touch is called a slap.
questions.source: English and Logical Reasoning Prep Guide

questions.subject: English
questions.examName: English and Logical Reasoning Prep Guide
questions.q: In each question below, are given two sentences numbered I and II. In these sentences, two homonyms are given in italics, which may be either misspelled or inappropriate in the context of the sentences. Read both the sentences carefully and decide on their correctness on the basis of the italicised words. I. This is the best stationary shop in our locality. II. I saw a stationery van there.
options: Both the sentences I and II are correct
I as well II are incorrect, but both could be made correct by interchanging the italicised words
questions.solution:
Both sentences are incorrect.

In the first sentence, the word 'stationary' is incorrect. It should be 'stationery' which means shops that contain writing materials such as paper, pen, envelopes, etc.

In the second sentence, the word 'stationery' is incorrect. It should be 'stationary' which is an adjective used for not moving objects.

It can be observed that both sentences can be corrected by interchanging the italicised words.
questions.source: English and Logical Reasoning Prep Guide

questions.subject: English
questions.examName: English and Logical Reasoning Prep Guide
questions.q:
Select the most appropriate homophone to fill in the blank.
Even animals find it difficult to \(\_\_\_\_\) the loss of their loved ones.
questions.solution:
- Bear means to endure or withstand, especially in the face of difficulty, loss, or grief.
- Example: She couldn't bear the pain of losing her pet.
- In the given sentence, the correct homophone is "bear."
- It conveys the idea that animals, like humans, struggle to endure or cope with the loss of their loved ones.
- Complete sentence: Even animals find it difficult to bear the loss of their loved ones.
questions.source: English and Logical Reasoning Prep Guide

questions.subject: English
questions.examName: English and Logical Reasoning Prep Guide
questions.q:
In each question below, are given two sentences numbered I and II. In these sentences, two homonyms are given in italics, which may be either misspelt or inappropriate in the context of the sentences. Read both the sentences carefully and decide on their correctness on the basis of the italicised words.

I. A vote of censur was passed against the President.
II. Before release, every film is sent to the censor office for a recommendation.
options: Both the sentences I and II are correct
I as well II are incorrect, but both could be made correct by interchanging the italicised words
questions.solution:
In the sentence I, the word 'censur' is incorrect. The correct word will be 'censure' which means a formal way of telling someone that he or she has done something wrong. Here, a formal vote against the President was passed.
In the sentence II, 'censor' is used which is a board that removes the parts of a movie that may offend people.
Hence, option B is the correct answer.
questions.source: English and Logical Reasoning Prep Guide

questions.subject: English
questions.examName: English and Logical Reasoning Prep Guide
questions.q:
In each question below, are given two sentences numbered I and II. In these sentences, two homonyms are given in italics, which may be either misspelt or inappropriate in the context of the sentences. Read both the sentences carefully and decide on their correctness on the basis of the italicised words.

I. This dress does not suit you.

II. He filed a civil suite against Mr. Arora.
options: Both the sentences I and II are correct
I as well II are incorrect, but both could be made correct by interchanging the italicised words
questions.solution:
The sentence I is correct. 'Suit' means something that looks good on someone. Here, a dress is not suiting a person.
In sentence II, the word 'suite' is used which is incorrect. It should be 'civil suit' which means a lawsuit that settles disagreement between private parties.
Hence, option A is the correct answer.
questions.source: English and Logical Reasoning Prep Guide

questions.subject: English
questions.examName: English and Logical Reasoning Prep Guide
questions.q:
In the question below, there are two sentences numbered I and II. In these sentences, two homophones are given in italics, which may be either misspelt or inappropriate in the context of the sentences. Read both the sentences carefully and decide on their correctness on the basis of the italicised words.

I. He drank a draut of wine.

II. Crops failed due to drout.
options: Both the sentences I and II are correct.
Neither I nor II is correct and the sentence could not be made correct by interchanging the italicised words.
questions.solution:
In the sentence I, the italicised word used is incorrect. It should be 'draught' which means the act of swallowing without taking a breath. Here, the subject drank the wine without taking a breath.
In the sentence II, the italicised word used is incorrect. It should be 'drought' which means the shortage of water supply. Here, the crops fail due to the shortage of water supply.
Hence, both the sentences are incorret.
questions.source: English and Logical Reasoning Prep Guide

questions.subject: English
questions.examName: English and Logical Reasoning Prep Guide
questions.q:
From the given options, find the pair which is similar to the given pair.

Given pair :- Davis Cup : Tennis
questions.solution:
The logic followed here is:

Logic: Association of Cups with Sports.

Here,

Davis Cup : Tennis \(\rightarrow\) Davis Cup is associated with the sport of Tennis.

Similarly,

Ryder Cup : Golf \(\rightarrow\) Ryder Cup is associated with the sport of Golf.

Hence, the correct answer is "Option 2".
questions.source: English and Logical Reasoning Prep Guide
